proposals.title: MaskHeadNet: Exploring Non-FCN Architectures for Instance Segmentation
proposals.motivation: In instance segmentation frameworks like Mask R-CNN, the mask head is typically a small, fully convolutional network (FCN). While effective, this design may be suboptimal for capturing complex spatial dependencies and long-range pixel relationships within an object's region of interest. Exploring alternative architectures for this critical component could unlock significant gains in mask accuracy and efficiency, a core challenge in computer vision.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing the standard FCN mask head with architectures explicitly designed for structured spatial reasoning, such as graph networks or lightweight vision transformers, will improve mask prediction quality and generalization, particularly for objects with intricate shapes or occlusions.
proposals.method: We will design a systematic search space of non-FCN architectures for the mask head, inspired by recent work on network design spaces and graph neural networks. Candidates will include a Graph Convolutional Network operating on superpixel nodes within the RoI, a compact axial-attention transformer block, and a U-Net with residual gated recurrent units. These modules will be integrated into the Mask R-CNN framework. We will employ a simple, label-efficient neural architecture search strategy, potentially unsupervised or self-supervised, to evaluate and rank architectures on COCO and LVIS datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive analysis of mask head design beyond FCNs, establishing new state-of-the-art baselines for mask prediction. It will yield practical, improved architectures for instance segmentation and contribute a principled design space for task-specific head networks in dense prediction tasks.
proposals.novelty: While architectural innovation has focused on backbones, this proposal systematically rethinks the specialized mask head, applying structured prediction models like GNNs to this localized task. The approach connects the design space philosophy to a critical, underexplored sub-network.

proposals.title: MaskFormer++: A Unified, Proposal-Free Framework for Instance Segmentation via Dense Feature Grouping
proposals.motivation: Current state-of-the-art instance segmentation methods, such as Mask R-CNN, rely heavily on a dedicated Region Proposal Network (RPN) to generate candidate object boxes before segmentation. This two-stage paradigm introduces complexity, computational overhead, and a potential bottleneck for detecting small or irregularly shaped instances. A simpler, unified architecture that directly segments instances from a dense feature map could improve efficiency and conceptual elegance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that instance segmentation can be effectively reformulated as a task of learning to group image pixels into a variable number of instance-aware clusters directly from a dense feature representation, thereby eliminating the need for an explicit RPN and bounding box prior.
proposals.method: We will build upon the MaskFormer architecture, which treats segmentation as a mask classification problem. Our core innovation is to replace its fixed set of learned object queries with a dynamic, content-aware query generation module. Inspired by unsupervised representation learning and graph methods, we will design a lightweight network head that operates on a high-resolution feature map from a backbone like a RegNet or Swin Transformer. This head will predict a set of 'instance seed' embeddings and pairwise affinity scores between pixels and seeds, enabling iterative grouping via a simple, differentiable clustering step (e.g., a few steps of masked attention). The final masks are decoded from the grouped features.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, end-to-end trainable instance segmentation framework that removes the RPN dependency, simplifying the model pipeline. We aim to demonstrate competitive accuracy on COCO with improved inference speed on dense prediction scenes. The research will also advance understanding of how to integrate unsupervised grouping concepts into supervised dense prediction tasks.
proposals.novelty: While query-based detectors like DETR exist, our approach uniquely focuses on generating instance-specific queries dynamically from pixel-level features for segmentation, not detection, and formalizes the grouping process as an integral, learnable component of the network. This moves beyond parallel mask prediction or top-down refinement used in contemporary single-stage segmenters.

proposals.title: MaskFormer++: End-to-End Instance Segmentation via Mask Set Prediction
proposals.motivation: Current state-of-the-art instance segmentation methods (e.g., Mask R-CNN) rely on a two-stage pipeline where bounding box detection precedes mask prediction. This explicit box regression step introduces a representational bottleneck and architectural complexity, potentially limiting performance and efficiency. A direct, box-free approach could simplify the pipeline and better capture the amorphous shapes of real-world objects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully designed neural network can directly predict a set of high-quality instance masks for an image in a single, end-to-end pass, without requiring an intermediate bounding box regression stage, and that this approach can match or exceed the accuracy of box-dependent methods.
proposals.method: We will build upon the DETR framework for set-based object detection, extending it to directly predict masks. The core architecture will be a Transformer encoder-decoder. The decoder will take a fixed set of learned object queries as input and output a set of mask embeddings. Each mask embedding will be used to dynamically generate parameters for a small, parallel mask prediction head (inspired by HyperNetworks and dynamic convolutions). These heads will operate on high-resolution feature maps from a backbone (e.g., a RegNet) to produce final instance masks. The model will be trained with a bipartite matching loss, directly optimizing mask quality via a combination of dice loss and focal loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, simplified paradigm for instance segmentation that removes the dependency on bounding boxes. It will deliver a fully end-to-end trainable model with a conceptually cleaner design space. We will empirically validate its performance on COCO and LVIS, aiming to set a new state-of-the-art for box-free methods and provide insights into the necessity of bounding boxes for segmentation.
proposals.novelty: While DETR removed the need for anchors and NMS for detection, its extension to segmentation still used box predictions. Our proposal is novel in completely bypassing the bounding box representation, using dynamic, query-conditioned mask heads to generate instance masks directly from a set of latent embeddings.

proposals.title: Mask R-CNN with Minimal Masks: A Self-Supervised and Semi-Supervised Design Space for Instance Segmentation
proposals.motivation: Mask R-CNN is a cornerstone for instance segmentation but requires extensive pixel-level annotations, which are costly and time-consuming to acquire. Recent advances in unsupervised representation learning and neural architecture search, as explored by He et al., have shown promise for reducing label dependence in other vision tasks. This project aims to directly apply these principles to the high-impact, annotation-heavy problem of instance segmentation.
proposals.hypothesis: By constructing a unified design space that integrates self-supervised pre-training objectives with a semi-supervised Mask R-CNN architecture, we can achieve performance comparable to fully-supervised baselines using only a small fraction of the annotated masks.
proposals.method: We will first design a self-supervised pre-training task for the Mask R-CNN backbone (e.g., ResNet-FPN) using a contrastive or pretext task on large-scale unlabeled video or image data, inspired by spatiotemporal representation learning studies. Subsequently, we will define a semi-supervised architecture design space that systematically explores augmentations, consistency regularization techniques (e.g., Mean Teacher), and a learnable label propagation module for the mask head. The search for optimal configurations within this space will leverage label-free neural architecture search principles to efficiently find effective data-efficient training recipes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, data-efficient training framework for Mask R-CNN, significantly lowering the annotation barrier for instance segmentation. It will also contribute a methodological blueprint for injecting unsupervised and semi-supervised learning into complex, multi-task network design spaces.
proposals.novelty: While semi-supervised learning exists for classification, its systematic integration into the multi-output design space of Mask R-CNN—particularly for mask prediction—is unexplored. The novelty lies in the co-design of self-supervised pre-training, semi-supervised fine-tuning architectures, and automated search for this specific, structured task.

proposals.title: MaskSelect: Unsupervised Neural Architecture Search for Lightweight Instance Segmentation on Mobile Devices
proposals.motivation: Instance segmentation models like Mask R-CNN are critical for mobile applications in AR and robotics, but their high computational cost prohibits real-time use on edge devices. While efficient backbones exist, the mask prediction head remains a significant bottleneck, and principled methods for co-designing and compressing the full two-stage architecture are lacking. This work addresses the need for a holistic, automated approach to creating deployable instance segmentation models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an unsupervised Neural Architecture Search (NAS) framework, guided by a novel graph-based representation of the mask head's structure, can discover drastically smaller and more efficient Mask R-CNN variants without sacrificing segmentation accuracy on standard benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will build upon the RegNet design space philosophy to define a structured search space for the mask head's feature pyramid and prediction modules. A graph neural network will encode architectural candidates, and an unsupervised controller, trained via reinforcement learning with only a latency/compute reward, will explore this space. The search will be coupled with a MobileNetV3 or EfficientNet-Lite backbone, and final models will be refined using spatiotemporal contrastive learning from videos to boost feature quality without box annotations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new family of ultra-efficient Mask R-CNN models benchmarked on mobile hardware. It will contribute a novel, unsupervised NAS methodology for structured prediction heads and demonstrate the utility of unsupervised spatiotemporal learning for efficient model fine-tuning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the joint, unsupervised co-design of the backbone and mask head within a structured search space, moving beyond isolated backbone efficiency or post-hoc pruning. Applying graph-based NAS and contrastive pre-training specifically to the mask head compression problem is unexplored.

proposals.title: Panoramic Mask R-CNN: A Simple, Unsupervised Design Space for Large-Scale Scene Understanding
proposals.motivation: Large-scale panoramic imagery from drones, satellites, and 360-degree cameras presents unique challenges for instance segmentation frameworks like Mask R-CNN. Standard models struggle with extreme aspect ratios, spherical distortions, and the computational burden of ultra-high-resolution inputs. Adapting to these modalities is crucial for applications in autonomous navigation, geospatial analysis, and immersive AR/VR, but current methods often rely on complex, task-specific pipelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a redesigned Mask R-CNN, built upon a simple and regularized network design space and leveraging unsupervised representation learning on panoramic data, can achieve robust, efficient instance segmentation without exhaustive manual annotation.
proposals.method: We will adapt Mask R-CNN by first exploring a novel backbone design space inspired by RegNet principles, optimized for processing multi-scale, distorted image patches. Instead of dense labels, we will pre-train this backbone using an unsupervised spatiotemporal learning objective on unlabeled panoramic video sequences, learning invariance to geometric distortions. For inference, we will employ a graph-based, adaptive tiling strategy that dynamically segments the panorama into manageable regions based on content complexity, minimizing redundant computation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable, end-to-end framework for panoramic instance segmentation that reduces reliance on labeled data. It will contribute a new, publicly available benchmark dataset for evaluating segmentation on large-scale 360-degree imagery. The proposed design principles will provide a blueprint for adapting other 2D perception models to extreme-resolution and spherical domains.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of an unsupervised, distortion-aware representation learning objective with a structured neural architecture search space specifically tailored for the unique properties of panoramas, moving beyond simple heuristic adaptations like equirectangular projection.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Synthesis of Realistic Images from Instance Masks via a Conditional Generative Design Space
proposals.motivation: Current image synthesis models like GANs and diffusion models are primarily driven by text or class labels, offering limited fine-grained control over object layout and scene composition. A method to generate realistic images directly from instance segmentation masks would provide a powerful tool for controllable content creation, data augmentation for vision tasks, and understanding the relationship between scene structure and appearance. This inverse problem remains underexplored, especially in an unsupervised setting without paired mask-image data.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural architecture, discovered through an unsupervised search within a structured design space conditioned on instance masks, can learn to synthesize photorealistic images that respect the input semantic and geometric layout without requiring labeled training pairs.
proposals.method: We will frame the problem as conditional image generation where the input is a set of instance masks (binary or semantic). The core architecture will be a conditional U-Net generator, but its specific connectivity, normalization layers, and upsampling blocks will be defined by a flexible design space, inspired by RegNet. We will train this system using a contrastive, unsupervised objective similar to SimCLR or MoCo, but applied across the image and mask domains: the network must learn to generate an image whose extracted features are invariant to augmentations and are predictive of the features from the original mask's structure. A graph neural network will encode the relational structure between mask instances to inform the generator.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel framework for structure-conditioned image synthesis that operates without paired supervision. It will advance the field of unsupervised representation learning by demonstrating its application to a complex conditional generation task. The resulting 'Generative Design Space' will provide architectural principles for mask-to-image models, extending the design space philosophy from discriminative to generative tasks.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines the principles of neural architecture design spaces and unsupervised contrastive learning for a structured conditional generation task, moving beyond typical GAN or VAE frameworks. It treats the mask-to-image mapping as an architecture search problem within an unsupervised learning paradigm, which is a novel synthesis of Dr. He's core research themes.

proposals.title: Searching for the Optimal Feature Hierarchy: A NAS-Driven Approach to Pyramid Backbone Design
proposals.motivation: Feature pyramid networks (FPNs) and their variants are fundamental for multi-scale vision tasks like object detection and segmentation. However, they are built upon fixed backbone hierarchies (e.g., ResNet's 4-stage design), which may be suboptimal for the specific pyramid construction and target task. This rigid coupling limits the potential for discovering more efficient and effective multi-scale representations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly searching for the optimal backbone stage definitions (e.g., number of stages, their depth, and downsampling factors) and the pyramid fusion pathways will yield architectures superior to those built on fixed, hand-designed backbones.
proposals.method: We will formulate a novel Neural Architecture Search (NAS) space that parameterizes both the backbone's hierarchical structure and the lateral connections for feature pyramid construction. Building on the principles of RegNet and the AnyNet design space, we will define a stochastic super-network where each 'stage' is a searchable block with variable resolution, width, and depth. We will employ a differentiable NAS (DARTS-like) strategy to efficiently co-search the backbone and pyramid modules end-to-end on a target task like COCO detection, using a task-specific performance metric as the search reward.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic framework for learning task-optimal feature hierarchies from data, moving beyond fixed backbone assumptions. It is expected to produce new, efficient backbone-pyramid co-designs that demonstrate improved accuracy-efficiency trade-offs on major vision benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While NAS has been used for backbone or neck design separately, co-searching the very definition of backbone stages alongside the pyramid structure is a novel exploration. This challenges the entrenched convention of pre-defined, sequential stages as the sole source of pyramidal features.

proposals.title: Multi-Scale Feature Pyramid Networks: A Unified Training Framework for Scale-Agnostic Feature Learning
proposals.motivation: Feature Pyramid Networks (FPNs) are a cornerstone of modern multi-scale object detection and segmentation systems. However, their training paradigm, inherited from standard backbones, typically relies on a single, fixed image scale per mini-batch, which may not optimally align the feature hierarchies across pyramid levels. This can lead to sub-optimal scale-invariant representations, as each level is tuned to a narrow range of scales seen during training. A unified, multi-scale training strategy could better exploit the FPN's inherent multi-resolution structure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training an FPN with multiple image scales presented simultaneously within a single forward/backward pass will enforce stronger and more consistent feature representations across pyramid levels, leading to improved scale generalization and robustness without increasing inference cost.
proposals.method: We will design a novel training batch composition where each input is a multi-scale image pyramid (e.g., 3-5 scales). These pyramids will be processed through a shared backbone and FPN in a single forward pass. A key technical challenge is designing a loss function that operates across all scales and pyramid levels. We will explore a consistency loss, inspired by unsupervised representation learning techniques, that encourages features for the same semantic content at different scales to be similar across corresponding FPN levels. This will be combined with standard supervised detection/segmentation losses. We will build upon and search within the design space of RegNet/ResNeXt backbones.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, principled training methodology for FPN-based architectures, potentially setting a new standard for multi-scale visual recognition. We expect to demonstrate measurable gains in metrics like Average Precision across scales on COCO and LVIS, particularly for small and large objects. The method will also offer insights into the relationship between input scale diversity and hierarchical feature learning.
proposals.novelty: While multi-scale testing and data augmentation are common, the core idea of *simultaneous* multi-scale training for FPNs, with an explicit cross-scale consistency objective, is unexplored. This moves beyond simple data augmentation to a structured learning objective that directly targets the FPN's architectural purpose.

proposals.title: Unified Neural Architecture Search for Joint Feature Extraction and Feature Pyramid Networks
proposals.motivation: Feature Pyramid Networks (FPNs) are crucial for multi-scale vision tasks like object detection, but their top-down pathway is typically designed and optimized separately from the backbone feature extractor. This decoupled design, while standard, may lead to sub-optimal feature hierarchies and limit overall model performance. Given the success of Neural Architecture Search (NAS) in discovering efficient backbones, a unified search space that jointly optimizes both components is a logical next step for holistic network design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly searching the architecture of the backbone and the FPN pathway in a unified, end-to-end optimization framework will discover more synergistic and task-optimal multi-scale representations than sequentially designed or fixed architectures.
proposals.method: We will extend the principles of 'Designing Network Design Spaces' to create a novel, unified search space encompassing both the backbone (e.g., cells based on ResNet/DARTS primitives) and the FPN's lateral connections, top-down pathway, and fusion modules. Using a label-efficient NAS method inspired by 'Are Labels Necessary for Neural Architecture Search?', we will employ unsupervised or self-supervised representation learning objectives (e.g., from contrastive learning on videos or images) to guide the search, reducing dependency on labeled data. The final architecture will be evaluated on downstream tasks like COCO object detection and instance segmentation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel NAS framework for discovering integrated backbone-FPN architectures and provide new insights into the co-design of feature extraction and pyramid building. The resulting architectures will establish a new state-of-the-art for efficient multi-scale visual recognition, demonstrating the value of holistic network optimization.
proposals.novelty: While NAS has been applied to backbones and some detection heads separately, the joint, end-to-end search of the backbone and the entire FPN pathway within a single, unsupervisedly-guided optimization loop is a novel and unexplored direction.

proposals.title: Beyond COCO: A Systematic Evaluation of Feature Pyramid Networks for Large-Scale and Long-Tailed Object Detection
proposals.motivation: The Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) has become a cornerstone module in modern object detectors, with its performance primarily validated on the COCO dataset. However, the real world presents greater scale and complexity, including datasets with millions of images and long-tailed class distributions. A rigorous, large-scale evaluation is needed to understand FPN's scalability, robustness, and potential failure modes beyond the curated COCO benchmark, informing the next generation of multi-scale feature architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while FPN provides a strong baseline, its fixed, heuristically-designed lateral connections and pyramid structure may exhibit suboptimal performance on larger-scale datasets with more extreme scale variation and long-tailed categories, revealing specific architectural bottlenecks.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled empirical study, integrating FPN into a standard detector backbone (e.g., ResNet) and training it on large-scale detection datasets available pre-2026, such as Open Images V6 and Objects365. We will systematically analyze performance across key axes: object scale distribution, instance count per category, and computational efficiency. The study will be complemented by diagnostic experiments probing the information flow across pyramid levels using gradient-based attribution methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive benchmark of FPN's performance on large-scale detection datasets, establishing new baselines for the community. It will yield concrete insights into the relationship between network design choices (like fusion operations) and dataset scale, guiding the development of more scalable and automated multi-scale architectures, potentially through neural architecture search.
proposals.novelty: While FPN is widely used, its systematic evaluation has been confined to COCO. This proposal shifts the focus to scalability and robustness under real-world data conditions, linking classic network design principles to emerging large-scale unsupervised and self-supervised learning paradigms where labeled detection data is sparse.

proposals.title: Dense Feature Pyramids: Enhancing Multi-Scale Representations via Dense Cross-Level Connections
proposals.motivation: Feature pyramids, as in FPN, are fundamental for multi-scale vision tasks like object detection and segmentation. However, the standard top-down pathway with lateral connections can suffer from information bottlenecks and gradient flow issues, limiting the richness of features at each scale. Improving feature flow across pyramid levels remains a critical architectural challenge for robust representation learning.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating dense connectivity patterns, inspired by DenseNet, between the layers of a feature pyramid network will significantly improve gradient flow and feature reuse, leading to more discriminative and robust multi-scale representations for downstream tasks.
proposals.method: We will design a Dense Feature Pyramid Network (DenseFPN) by replacing the standard lateral and top-down connections with dense blocks. Each pyramid level will receive concatenated feature maps from all preceding levels (both coarser and finer scales within the pyramid), not just its immediate predecessor. This will be integrated into a searchable design space, building on the RegNet methodology, to systematically explore efficient connection patterns and channel widths. The architecture will be evaluated in unsupervised (e.g., MoCo-v2 framework) and supervised settings on COCO and ImageNet.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, principled architecture for multi-scale visual recognition with demonstrably improved feature flow. It will provide empirical evidence on the benefits of dense connectivity in hierarchical networks and potentially establish a new strong baseline for tasks requiring feature pyramids. The design space exploration will yield insights into optimal connection patterns for multi-scale feature fusion.
proposals.novelty: While DenseNet connections are well-studied in backbone design, their systematic application to the inter-level connections of a feature pyramid network is novel. This moves beyond simply using a DenseNet backbone and instead re-architects the pyramid fusion mechanism itself, a non-obvious extension given the prevailing simplicity-focused design ethos post-ResNet/FPN.

proposals.title: Multi-Task Supervision for Learning Generalizable Pyramid Feature Representations
proposals.motivation: Feature pyramids, as in FPN, are crucial for multi-scale vision tasks but are typically learned implicitly through a single downstream objective (e.g., object detection). This can lead to features that are overly specialized and may not capture fundamental visual structures. Pre-2026, unsupervised representation learning has shown the value of pretext tasks, but their application to explicitly shape intermediate, multi-scale features within a supervised architecture remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: Jointly training pyramid features on auxiliary dense prediction tasks, such as semantic segmentation and edge detection, will force them to learn more general, geometrically-aware, and transferable representations, thereby improving performance and data efficiency on primary tasks like object detection and instance segmentation.
proposals.method: We will build upon a standard backbone (e.g., ResNet) and Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) design space. At each pyramid level, we will attach lightweight, task-specific heads for auxiliary self-supervised or weakly-supervised tasks, including edge detection (using a structured loss like BCE on Canny-like targets) and semantic segmentation (using a simple classifier). These auxiliary losses will be applied during primary task training (e.g., Mask R-CNN) with a gradient modulation strategy to balance their influence. The core architecture and training will be analyzed using the principles of network design space exploration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for injecting multi-task inductive biases into pyramid features, demonstrating improved generalization and robustness on COCO and Cityscapes benchmarks. It will yield insights into which auxiliary tasks most effectively regularize multi-scale features and offer a new perspective on 'designing' representations through auxiliary supervision rather than solely through architectural search.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning is well-studied, its targeted application to intermediate pyramid features—rather than final network outputs—to explicitly shape the foundational multi-scale representation is novel. This approach merges the design philosophy of FPN with the representation learning principles explored in unsupervised spatiotemporal studies, but applied within a fully supervised, multi-task pyramid context.

proposals.title: ST-FPN: A Simple Spatio-Temporal Feature Pyramid Network for Video Object Detection
proposals.motivation: Video object detection remains challenging due to motion blur, occlusion, and frame-to-frame appearance variations. While Feature Pyramid Networks (FPNs) have revolutionized spatial multi-scale feature learning for images, their direct application to video ignores the rich temporal context that can resolve ambiguities. A principled, simple extension of the FPN concept to the temporal dimension is needed to leverage this context efficiently.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending the spatial pyramid paradigm into a unified spatio-temporal pyramid, by constructing feature hierarchies across both space and time, will provide a more robust and efficient representation for detecting objects in video than processing frames independently or with complex, separate temporal modules.
proposals.method: We will design a Spatio-Temporal FPN (ST-FPN) that builds upon a standard 2D backbone (e.g., ResNet). The core innovation is a 'Temporal Pyramid Pooling' module inserted at each spatial pyramid level. This module will use simple, deformable temporal convolutions and cross-frame feature aggregation (e.g., via 3D convolutions or temporal self-attention) to construct features at multiple temporal receptive fields (e.g., short, medium, long-range). The architecture will be co-designed with a neural architecture search (NAS) strategy, inspired by 'Designing Network Design Spaces,' to find optimal connections between spatial and temporal pyramid levels.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a unified, conceptually simple framework for spatio-temporal feature learning in videos, directly extending the widely adopted FPN design. It will provide a strong baseline for video object detection that balances performance and efficiency. Furthermore, the resulting design space exploration will yield general principles for combining spatial and multi-scale temporal context.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, first-principles extension of the spatial pyramid to a joint spatio-temporal hierarchy within a single, learnable architecture, moving beyond ad-hoc temporal post-processing or complex two-stream designs. The integration of NAS to explore this new design space is also a novel application of He's prior work to the video domain.

proposals.title: Dynamic Depth Networks: Adaptive Computation via Learned Residual Gating
proposals.motivation: Modern deep networks apply a fixed, often excessive, computational budget to all inputs, leading to inefficiency. While conditional computation and early-exit networks exist, they often compromise the clean, scalable design principles of foundational architectures like ResNets. A method to dynamically adjust a network's effective depth per sample, without altering its core residual structure, would enable efficient inference while maintaining robust representational capacity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a simple, learnable gating mechanism can be embedded within a residual network design space to predictively route data through variable numbers of blocks, achieving adaptive computation without sacrificing the accuracy or trainability of the original architecture.
proposals.method: We will augment standard residual blocks with lightweight, input-dependent gating modules that output a binary halt/continue decision. These modules, inspired by the simplicity of Squeeze-and-Exitation layers, will be trained jointly with the network using a Gumbel-Softmax relaxation and a budget-aware loss term. The architecture search methodology from 'Designing Network Design Spaces' will be used to systematically explore the placement and design of these gates, ensuring the resulting dynamic network remains simple and scalable.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, principled design space for dynamic-depth residual networks that balances accuracy and efficiency. It will provide a framework for unsupervised or weakly-supervised discovery of adaptive computation policies, extending the concept of Neural Architecture Search to runtime behavior.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior early-exit works which attach side classifiers, our approach integrates the control mechanism directly into the residual flow, preserving its original functional mapping. The novelty lies in formulating dynamic depth as a constrained architecture search problem within a canonical, simple design space.

proposals.title: Collapsing the Detection Pipeline: A Unified Framework for Simultaneous Proposal and Recognition
proposals.motivation: Two-stage object detectors like Faster R-CNN have dominated high-accuracy benchmarks, but their sequential proposal-and-refinement design introduces inherent latency and complexity. The success of simpler, one-stage detectors like YOLO and RetinaNet on speed-critical tasks suggests a potential efficiency ceiling for two-stage paradigms. This work investigates whether this architectural separation is a fundamental necessity for high performance or an artifact of historical design, aiming to unify detection into a single, efficient, and equally accurate step.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that with a carefully designed neural architecture and training strategy, the proposal and detection stages can be collapsed into a single, simultaneous prediction step without sacrificing accuracy on complex detection benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will design a unified, dense prediction architecture inspired by RegNet principles, where a single, deeply fused head performs joint localization and classification. The core technical challenge is enabling this head to learn a structured, discretized representation of object locations and scales without explicit region proposals. We will explore graph neural networks to model spatial relationships between potential object anchors and employ unsupervised pre-training strategies, akin to those studied for spatiotemporal data, to learn robust visual representations that bootstrap the unified detection task.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive empirical analysis on the necessity of the two-stage paradigm, potentially leading to a new state-of-the-art in efficient, high-accuracy detection. It will contribute a novel, simplified network design space for object detection that bridges the gap between one-stage speed and two-stage accuracy.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically challenging a core tenet of modern detection architecture using a principled design-space exploration, rather than incremental improvement. It uniquely combines insights from neural architecture design, unsupervised representation learning, and graph-structured prediction within a single detection framework.

proposals.title: Task-Disentangled Feature Analysis: Are Shared Backbones Optimal for Object Detection?
proposals.motivation: Modern two-stage object detectors like Faster R-CNN rely on a single, shared convolutional backbone for both region proposal and object classification. This design is computationally efficient but assumes the optimal feature representation is identical for these distinct sub-tasks. Given the rise of specialized architectures and the findings from Neural Architecture Search (NAS) that task-specific structures can differ, this fundamental assumption requires rigorous testing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the feature representations optimal for the localization task (region proposal) are distinct from those optimal for the recognition task (final classification), and that a disentangled or task-adaptive feature architecture will outperform a rigid, shared backbone.
proposals.method: We will leverage the principles of network design spaces and unsupervised representation learning to construct a controlled experimental framework. Using a search space inspired by RegNet, we will perform a comprehensive Neural Architecture Search (NAS) to independently find optimal architectures for the Region Proposal Network (RPN) and the classification head. We will then analyze the divergence between these architectures and their learned features. Experiments will benchmark shared backbones against architectures with task-specific branches or dynamic feature routing, evaluated on COCO and a large-scale unsupervised video dataset to assess generalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale empirical evidence quantifying the potential sub-optimality of shared backbones in detection. It will yield concrete design principles for building more efficient and accurate detectors by explicitly considering task-specific feature needs, potentially influencing the next generation of architecture design.
proposals.novelty: While NAS has been applied to find single backbones, systematically searching for and comparing *task-specific* optimal architectures for the core components of a detector is novel. It directly challenges a ubiquitous but untested design convention.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Design of Fast, Very Deep Networks via Graph-Based Architecture Search
proposals.motivation: Very deep networks, such as those beyond VGG-16, are crucial for high-accuracy vision tasks but suffer from significant computational latency, hindering real-time applications. Current Neural Architecture Search (NAS) methods are often label-dependent and computationally expensive, focusing primarily on accuracy rather than inference speed for extreme depths. A systematic, unsupervised approach to co-design depth, topology, and efficiency is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating the network architecture as a computational graph and applying an unsupervised, latency-aware search within a structured design space, we can discover very deep network architectures that achieve substantially higher frame rates without sacrificing representational capacity.
proposals.method: We will extend the principles of 'Designing Network Design Spaces' and unsupervised NAS ('Are Labels Necessary?') to construct a graph-based search space over very deep, non-residual macro-architectures. Using a simple, lightweight proxy task from spatiotemporal representation learning, we will perform a gradient-free search optimized directly for a latency objective on target hardware. The core innovation is a structured pruning and connection learning algorithm that operates on the initial overly-deep graph to learn an optimal, faster sub-graph.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new family of very deep, high-speed network architectures validated on large-scale video datasets. It will provide a methodology for hardware-aware, unsupervised architecture search that decouples design from labeled data. The resulting models and design space analysis will offer insights into the efficiency bottlenecks of depth.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the joint application of unsupervised NAS and graph-based structure learning specifically for latency optimization in very deep networks, moving beyond residual patterns and supervised accuracy-centric search.

proposals.title: Unified Visual Task Learning via a Simple, Graph-Structured Task Interaction Network
proposals.motivation: Current computer vision systems often treat tasks like object detection and segmentation as separate, requiring distinct models or multi-stage pipelines. This leads to inefficiency, redundancy, and suboptimal feature sharing. A truly unified, end-to-end training framework for core visual tasks is needed to improve performance, reduce computational cost, and simplify deployment, aligning with the trend towards more general-purpose vision models.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, end-to-end trainable network with a unified representation and a learnable, graph-structured interaction module can jointly and effectively perform region proposal, object detection, and instance segmentation, outperforming separate or sequentially trained models.
proposals.method: We will design a unified architecture based on a shared backbone (e.g., a RegNet variant). A dense, pixel-level feature map will be processed by a novel, lightweight Graph Neural Network (GNN) module where nodes represent potential task-specific outputs (e.g., boxes, masks, classes). The GNN's edges will model task interactions and be learned in an unsupervised or self-supervised manner, inspired by label-free NAS principles. The entire system will be trained end-to-end with a multi-task loss combining localization, classification, and segmentation objectives.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a simple, efficient, and high-performing unified model for core recognition tasks. It will provide new insights into how different vision tasks can mutually benefit from joint, structured learning within a single forward pass, moving beyond simple weight sharing.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning exists, our approach uniquely integrates unsupervised graph structure learning to dynamically model task relationships within a fully end-to-end detection and segmentation pipeline, avoiding hand-designed task hierarchies or fixed fusion schemes.

proposals.title: Generative Region Proposal Networks: Image Synthesis via Object-Level Layout Priors
proposals.motivation: Current image generation models, such as GANs and diffusion models, learn holistic scene composition implicitly, often struggling with coherent object-level structure. Conversely, Region Proposal Networks (RPNs) in object detection explicitly learn powerful, low-dimensional representations of object layout and geometry. This project investigates whether this explicit spatial understanding can be inverted to guide or directly perform realistic image generation, bridging a gap between discriminative and generative vision models.
proposals.hypothesis: The latent space of a trained RPN encodes a sufficiently rich and disentangled prior over object layout and basic appearance that it can be used as a conditioning mechanism or a direct generative model for synthesizing images with coherent object structure.
proposals.method: We will design a Generative RPN (GRPN) by attaching a decoder network to the feature maps and proposal outputs of a frozen, pre-trained RPN (e.g., from Mask R-CNN). The decoder, based on a simple U-Net or similar architecture, will be trained to reconstruct the original image given the RPN's region features and bounding box coordinates. We will explore training this system in an unsupervised manner using a reconstruction loss, and later condition it on latent codes to generate novel layouts and images. The approach will be evaluated on datasets like COCO for object-centric generation and completion tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel paradigm for object-aware image generation, directly leveraging discriminative architectural primitives. It will provide new insights into the invertibility of representation learning in detection networks and could yield a simple, efficient method for layout-to-image synthesis that requires no explicit segmentation maps or graphical models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses detection outputs to condition separate GANs, we propose to make the RPN itself a generative component by inverting its forward pass. This directly connects the 'network design spaces' philosophy to generative modeling in a way not previously explored, treating the proposal mechanism as a learned spatial prior for synthesis.

proposals.title: Dual-Stream Networks: Learning to Suppress Backgrounds for Unsupervised Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Current unsupervised representation learning methods, especially in vision, often learn features by treating all image regions equally. However, background clutter can act as a confounding factor, limiting the quality of learned object-centric representations. Explicitly modeling and suppressing non-object regions could provide a stronger, more focused learning signal, analogous to how attention mechanisms improve supervised models.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural network can learn to actively predict and suppress background regions in an unsupervised manner, and this 'inverse' objective will force the model to develop more robust, object-centric representations than standard reconstruction or contrastive losses alone.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-stream architecture built upon a simple, scalable backbone like a RegNet. The primary stream performs a standard unsupervised task (e.g., masked autoencoding or temporal contrastive learning). The secondary 'suppression' stream takes a corrupted version of the input (e.g., with foreground regions masked) and is trained to reconstruct the predicted background mask from the primary stream's latent features. This creates a dual learning objective: the primary stream must implicitly segment the object to provide clean features for the secondary stream's background prediction task.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new, generic framework for incorporating spatial grounding into unsupervised learning without labels. It will provide empirical evidence on large-scale datasets (e.g., ImageNet-1K, Kinetics) that background suppression is a powerful self-supervised signal, potentially leading to state-of-the-art transfer performance on downstream tasks like detection and segmentation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses saliency or attention to focus on objects, we explicitly formulate the learning of background regions as a primary, auxiliary task. This inverts the typical object-proposal paradigm and integrates it seamlessly into an unsupervised network design space, moving beyond hand-crafted pretext tasks.

proposals.title: Unpaired Image Collections for Super-Resolution: A Simple, Unsupervised Design Space
proposals.motivation: Supervised single-image super-resolution (SISR) relies on perfectly aligned low-resolution (LR) and high-resolution (HR) image pairs, which are difficult and expensive to acquire for real-world data. This constraint limits the applicability of learned SR to diverse, real-world domains like historical photos or specialized microscopy. Unsupervised learning from readily available, unpaired LR and HR image collections could dramatically broaden the scope and practicality of SR systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a simple, carefully designed neural architecture and training objective can learn an effective SR mapping by exploiting statistical and structural consistency between unpaired LR and HR image sets, without requiring pixel-level correspondence.
proposals.method: We will design a new network design space for unpaired SR, building on principles of simplicity from ResNet and RegNet. The core method adapts cycle-consistency concepts from unpaired image translation (e.g., CycleGAN) but within a structured, single-task SR framework. We will formulate a composite loss enforcing cycle-consistency on the downscaled HR path, perceptual similarity within the HR manifold, and a simple adversarial loss to align the distributions of generated and real HR patches. Neural Architecture Search (NAS) will be used to discover optimal architectures within this constrained design space, guided by unsupervised metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, practical paradigm for training super-resolution models on uncurated, real-world image data. It will provide a systematic analysis of architectural choices for unpaired SR through a released design space. The resulting models will demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks where aligned training pairs are unavailable.
proposals.novelty: While unpaired translation is known, its principled application to the ill-posed, structured task of super-resolution—combined with a neural architecture design space exploration—is novel. This moves beyond simple adaptation of existing GAN frameworks to a dedicated, architecture-aware solution for SR.

proposals.title: Residual Learning for Unsupervised Super-Resolution: A Design Space Analysis
proposals.motivation: Single-image super-resolution (SISR) is a fundamental low-level vision task, but training deep networks for it remains challenging due to the difficulty of learning a direct mapping from low-resolution (LR) to high-resolution (HR) images. While residual learning, popularized by ResNet, has revolutionized high-level vision tasks by easing the optimization of very deep networks, its principled application to the SISR problem space—where the goal is to predict a residual (the high-frequency detail)—remains underexplored in a systematic, unsupervised context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly structuring a super-resolution network to learn the residual detail between bicubic-upsampled LR and HR images, within a carefully designed search space, will yield more stable training, enable the use of deeper architectures, and improve performance, particularly when leveraging unsupervised or weakly-supervised data.
proposals.method: We will design a new neural architecture search (NAS) space for SISR based on the principles of 'Designing Network Design Spaces.' This space will parameterize the placement, type (e.g., local, global, multi-scale), and aggregation of residual connections. Building on 'Are Labels Necessary for Neural Architecture Search?', we will employ unsupervised contrastive or reconstruction-based objectives to evaluate architectures within this space efficiently. The final model will be a deep network where the primary output is the residual detail, added to an interpolated LR input.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, principled design space for residual-based super-resolution networks, validated through large-scale experiments. It will provide empirical evidence on how residual learning mechanics transfer from high-level to low-level vision. The resulting architectures and unsupervised search methodology will advance the state-of-the-art in efficient and scalable SISR model development.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic co-design of a residual-centric NAS space specifically for the super-resolution task, coupled with an unsupervised evaluation strategy. This moves beyond simply using a ResNet backbone for SISR to fundamentally re-architect the problem around residual learning from the ground up.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Visual Representation Learning via Perceptual Feature Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Current unsupervised representation learning methods, such as contrastive learning, excel at semantic tasks but often produce features that are suboptimal for fine-grained visual quality and perceptual similarity. This limits their application in domains like image generation, super-resolution, and style transfer, where perceptual metrics are crucial. A method that can learn representations directly aligned with human visual perception, without labeled data, would bridge a significant gap in the field.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by using a perceptual loss derived from the feature visualizations of a fixed, pre-trained network as a self-supervised training signal, we can learn visual representations that inherently prioritize perceptual quality and structural coherence, surpassing purely semantic contrastive objectives.
proposals.method: We will design a simple, non-contrastive learning framework where a student encoder is trained to reconstruct the feature maps of a fixed, pre-trained teacher network (e.g., a ResNet trained on ImageNet) from augmented views of an image. The key innovation is to compute the reconstruction loss not on raw feature activations, but on the *feature visualizations* (e.g., via activation maximization or feature inversion) of selected high-level teacher neurons. This forces the student network to internalize the visual patterns that the teacher deems salient. We will explore this within a structured network design space, automating the search for architectures best suited to this perceptual objective.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, label-free objective for learning perceptually-aligned visual representations. It will provide a systematic analysis of how feature visualization can serve as a supervisory signal, extending the principles of network design spaces to perceptual learning. The resulting models are expected to offer superior performance on downstream tasks requiring visual fidelity.
proposals.novelty: While perceptual losses are used in supervised image restoration, applying them as a primary, unsupervised objective for representation learning via feature visualization is unexplored. This moves beyond matching feature statistics to directly synthesizing the visual concepts that features represent, creating a novel link between feature visualization and self-supervised learning.

proposals.title: RegNet for Gigapixels: A Simple, Unsupervised Design Space for Efficient Ultra-High-Resolution Image Reconstruction
proposals.motivation: Processing gigapixel images, such as satellite maps or whole-slide microscopy, is computationally prohibitive for current super-resolution (SR) networks, which are designed for standard resolutions (e.g., 2K). Existing methods often rely on complex, handcrafted multi-scale architectures or patch-based processing with limited global context. A principled, efficient, and scalable neural design for direct ultra-high-resolution (UHR) processing is an open challenge in computer vision.
proposals.hypothesis: A regularized design space, derived from principles of simple, efficient networks like RegNet, can be optimized for unsupervised representation learning to enable memory-efficient, end-to-end super-resolution of gigapixel-scale images, outperforming complex, manually designed UHR architectures.
proposals.method: We will adapt the RegNet design space methodology to search for optimal, lightweight CNN blocks for UHR processing. The core innovation is a hierarchical, graph-structured processing pipeline where a global context network at low resolution guides localized, high-resolution refinement networks applied to dynamically tiled regions. Training will use an unsupervised objective based on temporal or multi-view consistency from video or satellite time-series data, avoiding costly high-res labeled pairs. The search will optimize for metrics balancing reconstruction fidelity (PSNR/SSIM) and memory/FLOPs on simulated gigapixel data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) A novel, scalable neural architecture design space tailored for UHR image reconstruction, 2) An unsupervised training framework for SR that leverages readily available temporal data, and 3) Benchmark results and models for gigapixel-scale processing, setting a new standard for efficiency and quality.
proposals.novelty: While RegNet design spaces exist for classification and NAS is used for SR, their joint application to *unsupervised*, *gigapixel-scale* SR via a learned hierarchical graph structure is unexplored. This moves beyond patch-based or simple scaling of existing SR networks.

proposals.title: Learning to Downscale: A Neural Architecture Search Framework for Optimal Low-Resolution Image Generation
proposals.motivation: Super-resolution research overwhelmingly focuses on the upscaling task, treating the low-resolution (LR) image as a given, often degraded by a simple kernel like bicubic. This ignores a critical inverse problem: what is the optimal downscaling operator that preserves maximal information for a subsequent upscaling network? Solving this is crucial for real-world pipelines where the LR image is actively created (e.g., in rendering, streaming, or efficient sensing).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a downscaling operator, co-designed with a specific upscaling network via a unified neural architecture search, can produce LR images that are significantly more "upscalable," leading to superior reconstruction fidelity compared to using fixed, hand-crafted downsamplers.
proposals.method: We will frame the problem as a bilevel optimization. An outer loop, guided by principles from 'Designing Network Design Spaces', will search over a structured space of lightweight, parameterized downscaling architectures (e.g., using strided convolutions with learned filters). The inner loop will train a fixed, state-of-the-art upscaling network (e.g., an EDSR or RCAN variant) on the LR outputs from the candidate downscaler. The search objective will maximize the PSNR/SSIM of the reconstructed HR image on a validation set, using gradient-based or weight-sharing NAS techniques explored in 'Are Labels Necessary for Neural Architecture Search?'. The downscaler will be constrained to a fixed compression ratio.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, learnable framework for optimal image downscaling tailored for reconstruction. It will yield a principled method to generate 'best-possible' LR proxies for any upscaling network. We will release a family of learned downscaling operators that can serve as a new standard preprocessing step for super-resolution benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses fixed degradation models, we treat the downscaling operator itself as a learnable network architecture to be discovered, inverting the typical super-resolution paradigm. The core novelty is the co-design of the downscaling and upscaling processes through NAS, moving beyond isolated network design.

proposals.title: Unified Non-Local Networks: A Systematic Study of Long-Range Dependencies Across Vision, Graph, and Sequential Domains
proposals.motivation: The non-local block, introduced for video classification, effectively captures long-range spatiotemporal dependencies but has seen limited exploration beyond core vision tasks. Given the fundamental challenge of modeling long-range interactions in diverse data structures—from point clouds to protein graphs and time series—a systematic investigation is needed. This work aims to determine if the non-local operation is a general-purpose primitive for relational reasoning, which could simplify architecture design across fields.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the non-local block, as a generic building block for capturing pairwise dependencies, can be effectively and efficiently integrated into network architectures for non-vision domains, leading to consistent performance improvements on tasks requiring relational reasoning.
proposals.method: We will conduct a large-scale empirical study, integrating non-local blocks into canonical backbones for three domains: 1) Graph Neural Networks (e.g., GCN, GAT) for molecular property prediction, 2) 3D point cloud networks (e.g., PointNet++) for segmentation, and 3) Temporal Convolutional Networks for long-sequence forecasting. We will adapt the interaction space (e.g., using edge features for graphs, temporal ordering for sequences) and employ the efficient 'criss-cross' or 'bottleneck' variants from vision to manage computational cost. Performance will be benchmarked against strong baselines and alternative attention mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive evaluation of non-local networks as a cross-domain architectural component, establishing empirical best practices for their use. It will contribute a unified perspective on capturing long-range dependencies, potentially reducing the fragmentation between vision, graph, and sequence modeling communities. The released models and design guidelines will serve as a strong baseline for future research.
proposals.novelty: While non-local blocks are established in vision, their application and rigorous evaluation in graph and sequential data domains is novel. The systematic comparison across fundamentally different data modalities, using a consistent methodological framework, moves beyond incremental application to provide a principled understanding of its generalizability.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Segmentation via Inverted Global Affinity: Localizing Distinct Regions from Non-Local Context
proposals.motivation: Current unsupervised representation learning excels at capturing global context and object-level semantics, but often lacks explicit mechanisms for fine-grained spatial segmentation. Conversely, classical segmentation methods rely heavily on pixel-level local cues or supervised data. A principled, unsupervised method to derive precise local segmentations directly from learned global feature affinities would bridge this gap, enabling structure discovery without labels.
proposals.hypothesis: The affinity matrix derived from a non-local or transformer module in a self-supervised network encodes sufficient relational structure to be analytically inverted, producing a saliency map that highlights locally distinct regions, thereby enabling unsupervised segmentation.
proposals.method: We will build upon a self-supervised vision backbone (e.g., MoCo-v2 or a Vision Transformer pre-trained via DINO) and extract the affinity matrix from its final non-local or self-attention layer. We will then formulate the inversion as an optimization problem: minimizing the consistency between a proposed binary mask and the global affinity patterns, such that regions with high internal affinity but low affinity to the background are identified. This will be operationalized through a novel, parameter-free algorithm that processes the affinity matrix to yield a segmentation energy map, followed by iterative refinement within a simple, learned network head to produce final segment proposals.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new paradigm for unsupervised segmentation that is directly derived from global context models, moving beyond pixel-level clustering. It will provide a theoretical and practical link between self-supervised representation learning and dense prediction tasks. The resulting method will be evaluated on complex scene datasets (e.g., COCO) without using their labels, demonstrating segmentation quality approaching supervised baselines.
proposals.novelty: While non-local networks and self-attention are used to propagate global context, using the resulting affinity matrix explicitly and inversely to *find* local segments is a novel, counter-intuitive direction. The approach is distinct from prior clustering or saliency detection methods as it is analytically derived from the model's own relational reasoning, not low-level image statistics.

proposals.title: MoCo-3D: Momentum Contrast for Unsupervised Representation Learning on 3D Point Clouds
proposals.motivation: The success of contrastive learning frameworks like MoCo in 2D vision has not been fully realized for 3D geometric data. Unsupervised pre-training for 3D point clouds remains a significant challenge, as existing methods often rely on handcrafted pretext tasks or complex generative models. A robust, MoCo-style framework could provide a simple, scalable, and effective pathway to learn transferable representations for 3D tasks like object classification and part segmentation, reducing dependency on large labeled datasets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core principles of the Momentum Contrast (MoCo) framework—maintaining a large, consistent dictionary of negative samples via a momentum-updated encoder—can be effectively adapted to learn powerful, transferable representations from unlabeled 3D point cloud data.
proposals.method: We will adapt the MoCo-v2 framework to operate on 3D point clouds. The query and key encoders will be based on architectures like PointNet++ or DGCNN. We will design a suite of 3D-specific data augmentations (e.g., random scaling, rotation, jittering, and point dropout) to generate positive pairs. The model will be pre-trained on large-scale unlabeled 3D datasets like ShapeNet. We will then evaluate the learned representations by fine-tuning on downstream tasks including 3D object classification (ModelNet40) and part segmentation (ShapeNetPart).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a simple and strong baseline for unsupervised representation learning on 3D point clouds, extending the proven MoCo paradigm into a new modality. It will provide empirical evidence on the transferability of contrastive learning principles across data structures and establish a new state-of-the-art for unsupervised pre-training on major 3D benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While MoCo has been applied to 2D images and video, its direct application to unordered, non-Euclidean 3D point cloud data is non-trivial and unexplored. The novelty lies in the systematic adaptation of the framework, including the design of effective 3D augmentations and the integration with graph-based neural architectures for point clouds.

proposals.title: MoCo-Gen: A Unified Framework for Contrastive and Generative Unsupervised Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, unsupervised visual representation learning is dominated by two distinct paradigms: contrastive methods like MoCo, which excel at discrimination, and generative methods like masked autoencoders (MAE), which excel at capturing detailed pixel-level structure. Combining their complementary strengths could yield more powerful and general-purpose representations, a direction not yet fully explored. This is crucial for reducing the dependency on labeled data and building foundational visual models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating the instance discrimination objective of Momentum Contrast (MoCo) with a generative pretext task, such as image inpainting or masked prediction, within a single, co-designed network will produce visual representations that surpass those from either method alone in downstream task performance.
proposals.method: We will design a dual-branch network architecture where one branch executes the standard MoCo v3 framework for contrastive learning. The second branch will incorporate a lightweight decoder to perform a generative task on masked or corrupted versions of the same image. A shared encoder, based on a Vision Transformer (ViT) or a carefully designed CNN from RegNet design spaces, will be trained with a combined loss function: the InfoNCE contrastive loss and a reconstruction loss (e.g., L2 or perceptual loss). Key challenges include balancing the two losses and designing an efficient, non-conflicting gradient flow.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, unified framework for unsupervised learning that leverages both discriminative and generative signals. We expect to demonstrate state-of-the-art transfer performance on ImageNet classification, COCO object detection, and semantic segmentation. The study will also provide insights into the synergies and trade-offs between these two major learning paradigms.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored multi-task learning in unsupervised settings, a principled co-design and joint optimization of a momentum-based contrastive system with a strong generative pretext task is novel. The integration focuses on architectural and optimization synergy rather than simple sequential pre-training or loss addition.

proposals.title: MoCo-Adapt: Resource-Adaptive End-to-End Contrastive Learning with Performance Guarantees
proposals.motivation: End-to-end training of Momentum Contrast (MoCo) removes the memory queue but incurs significant computational cost from maintaining a large number of negative samples through backpropagation. Current methods use static resource allocation, leading to inefficient training when fixed high-cost settings are unnecessary. A dynamic, performance-aware framework is needed to reduce this cost while provably preventing catastrophic collapse or quality degradation.
proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight auxiliary network performing a self-supervised proxy task can reliably monitor representation quality in real-time, enabling a controller to dynamically reduce computational factors (e.g., negatives, gradient paths) without dropping final task accuracy below a guaranteed floor.
proposals.method: We integrate a small, fixed-capacity auxiliary network (e.g., a 2-layer MLP) performing a simple pretext task (e.g., rotation prediction) on the primary encoder's outputs. This network provides a continuous quality signal. A controller, modeled as a lightweight policy network, uses this signal to adjust parameters of the end-to-end MoCo training: the number of in-batch negative samples propagated, the frequency of gradient updates through the momentum encoder, and the depth of projection head layers used. The controller is trained with a reward function that penalizes resource use but heavily penalizes drops in the auxiliary task performance below a set threshold.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel resource-adaptive framework for self-supervised learning that provides a formal performance guarantee. 2) A significant reduction in the compute and memory footprint of end-to-end MoCo training without sacrificing downstream accuracy. 3) A general methodology for using auxiliary proxy tasks for real-time training monitoring and control.
proposals.novelty: While neural architecture search and design spaces optimize static structures, this work dynamically optimizes the *training process* itself for efficiency. The integration of a performance-monitoring auxiliary network for closed-loop control of contrastive learning parameters is a novel departure from static, hand-designed training recipes.

proposals.title: Scaling Momentum Contrast (MoCo) for Unsupervised Pre-training on Billion-Scale Datasets
proposals.motivation: The Momentum Contrast (MoCo) framework has established state-of-the-art results in unsupervised visual representation learning on ImageNet. However, the scaling laws and performance limits of such contrastive methods remain poorly understood, as they have not been systematically evaluated on datasets orders of magnitude larger than ImageNet. This investigation is critical to determine if unsupervised pre-training can consistently outperform supervised pre-training as data scales, a key question for the future of computer vision.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling MoCo pre-training to billion-scale, web-curated image datasets will yield representations with significantly improved transfer performance across diverse downstream tasks, and that the relationship between dataset scale and performance follows a predictable, sub-linear scaling law.
proposals.method: We will adapt the MoCo v2 framework for efficient training on datasets like JFT-3B or a curated subset of Instagram images (~1B images). Key technical adaptations include: 1) implementing a sharded, distributed dynamic memory bank to manage the massive negative sample queue across hundreds of GPUs, 2) exploring more efficient encoder architectures from the RegNet design space to reduce computational cost, and 3) systematically ablating key hyperparameters (queue size, momentum coefficient, batch size) at scale. We will benchmark transfer performance on a suite of tasks including ImageNet classification, COCO object detection, and ADE20K semantic segmentation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive empirical study of contrastive learning at an unprecedented data scale, establishing new benchmarks for unsupervised representation learning. It will yield practical insights and optimized configurations for large-scale self-supervised training, and rigorously test the hypothesis that data scale can reduce or eliminate the gap between unsupervised and supervised pre-training.
proposals.novelty: While MoCo has been scaled to larger batches, systematically scaling it to billion-image datasets with the necessary distributed systems engineering and a rigorous analysis of scaling laws is a novel and unexplored direction. The work uniquely combines Kaiming He's expertise in network design spaces (RegNet) and unsupervised learning (MoCo) to tackle this scaling challenge.

proposals.title: Fully Convolutional Object Detection: A Unified Architecture Without Per-Region Computation
proposals.motivation: Modern two-stage detectors (e.g., Faster R-CNN) and many one-stage detectors rely on per-region-of-interest (RoI) operations, such as RoI pooling or cropping, which introduce computational overhead and architectural complexity. This limits inference speed and creates a disconnect between feature extraction and final prediction. A detector that operates entirely on a shared, dense convolutional feature map could be simpler, faster, and more amenable to unified optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully designed, fully convolutional network can perform accurate object detection by directly predicting bounding boxes and class scores from a shared feature map, completely eliminating the need for any per-region feature computation or sampling after the backbone.
proposals.method: We will design a new detection head architecture inspired by the simplicity of fully convolutional networks for segmentation. The core is a dense prediction module that outputs, for each spatial location on a high-resolution feature map, a fixed set of predictions (class, box offset, objectness). To handle scale variation without RoIAlign, we will leverage a multi-scale feature pyramid structure (FPN) and explore novel, lightweight sub-networks for feature refinement and prediction at each pyramid level. The training will use a dense, bipartite matching loss (inspired by DETR's set prediction) but within a fully convolutional framework to avoid sequential processing.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel object detection architecture that is conceptually simpler and computationally more efficient by removing a major bottleneck. It will advance the field by demonstrating that high accuracy can be achieved with a purely dense, per-pixel prediction paradigm, bridging the gap between segmentation and detection frameworks. The resulting model will serve as a strong baseline for efficient detection.
proposals.novelty: While FCOS and similar works reduce per-region computation, they still involve complex post-processing and heuristic assignment of locations to objects. Our approach is novel in its pursuit of a *strictly zero* per-RoI computation model after the backbone, combined with a unified, set-based loss applied directly to dense convolutional outputs, moving beyond the anchor-box or center-based paradigms prevalent pre-2026.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Design Spaces for Deep Fully Convolutional Backbones
proposals.motivation: The success of ResNet variants and other fully convolutional networks (FCNs) in supervised tasks is well-established, but their potential in unsupervised representation learning remains underexplored. Recent work by He et al. (2020) on unsupervised spatiotemporal learning and neural architecture search (NAS) without labels suggests that architectural choices are crucial even without supervision. A systematic investigation of deeper and novel FCN backbones in this context could unlock new performance frontiers for unsupervised visual and spatiotemporal tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematically scaling the depth and complexity of fully convolutional backbones, guided by principles from network design spaces and unsupervised learning objectives, will yield significantly richer and more transferable visual representations than those from standard ResNet-50 models commonly used in pre-2026 self-supervised learning.
proposals.method: We will leverage the RegNet design space methodology to construct a new, expanded design space focused on deeper and more complex FCNs, including variants beyond standard ResNet structures. These architectures will be trained using state-of-the-art unsupervised objectives, such as contrastive learning (e.g., MoCo v2) or clustering-based methods (e.g., SwAV), on large-scale image and video datasets. We will then perform a rigorous empirical study, analyzing the relationship between architectural factors (depth, width, group convolutions) and downstream task performance (e.g., ImageNet linear evaluation, object detection, video action recognition).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale empirical analysis of deep FCN backbones for unsupervised learning, establishing new architectural best practices. It will deliver a set of high-performing, pre-trained backbone models that advance the state-of-the-art in unsupervised representation learning. Furthermore, it will extend the principles of automated network design spaces into the unsupervised domain.
proposals.novelty: While design spaces have been explored for supervised tasks, their application to systematically discover optimal architectures for unsupervised learning is novel. The explicit focus on pushing beyond the conventional ResNet-50 depth and exploring novel FCN structures within a principled design space for self-supervised learning is a non-obvious and unexplored direction.

proposals.title: Deep-R-FCN: A Unified Training Framework for Optimizing Very Deep Object Detectors
