proposals.motivation: Region-based Fully Convolutional Networks (R-FCN) offer efficient object detection, but their performance with extremely deep backbones (e.g., ResNet-200, ResNeXt) is often suboptimal due to training instability and gradient degradation. As the field pushes for higher accuracy through depth, specialized training techniques are needed to unlock the full potential of these deep architectures in a fully convolutional detection setting, a problem less studied than for image classification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a co-designed training framework, integrating principles from network design spaces and unsupervised representation learning, can stabilize optimization and significantly improve the accuracy of R-FCN with very deep backbones, without requiring architectural modifications to the core detector.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a large-scale ablation study using a design-of-experiments approach, inspired by 'Designing Network Design Spaces', to systematically evaluate training hyperparameters (e.g., optimizer, learning rate schedules, weight initialization) for R-FCN with backbones deeper than ResNet-152. We will then integrate and adapt techniques from unsupervised spatiotemporal learning, such as momentum-based teacher networks and progressive contrastive loss warm-up, to pre-train and fine-tune the backbone features specifically for the detection task. Finally, we will explore a simple, learnable gating mechanism on the lateral connections within the Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) to dynamically regulate gradient flow.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a reproducible, open-source training recipe for state-of-the-art, very deep R-FCN detectors. It will provide new insights into the optimization landscape of deep detection networks and demonstrate how self-supervised pre-training paradigms can be tailored for downstream tasks beyond classification.
proposals.novelty: While deep network training is well-studied for classification, a systematic, unified framework for deep *object detection* training is lacking. The novelty lies in the explicit co-adaptation of unsupervised learning strategies and structured hyperparameter search from design spaces to solve the specific optimization challenges of R-FCN.

proposals.title: Capsule Routing for Unsupervised Neural Architecture Search in Pose Estimation
proposals.motivation: Accurate object pose estimation is crucial for robotics and AR/VR, but current deep learning methods often lack explicit modeling of part-whole hierarchies and viewpoint equivariance, leading to fragility under novel poses. Capsule networks, with their dynamic routing-by-agreement mechanism, offer a principled way to model such relationships, but their integration into modern, scalable neural architecture search (NAS) frameworks remains underexplored, limiting their practical impact.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating capsule network routing mechanisms into an unsupervised neural architecture search framework will yield pose-aware network architectures that are more robust and data-efficient for 6-DoF object pose estimation than standard convolutional designs.
proposals.method: We will extend the principles from 'Designing Network Design Spaces' and unsupervised NAS ('Are Labels Necessary for Neural Architecture Search?') to create a new design space where the fundamental building block is a capsule layer with dynamic routing. We will develop an unsupervised objective, building on spatiotemporal representation learning ('A Large-Scale Study on Unsupervised Spatiotemporal Representation Learning'), that rewards the discovery of architectures where routing agreement correlates with geometric consistency across synthetic viewpoint transformations. The search will be performed over graph-based representations of capsule connectivity and routing iterations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, principled design space for pose-aware neural architectures discovered through unsupervised NAS. It will provide empirical evidence on the scalability and effectiveness of capsule-inspired mechanisms in a structured search paradigm, potentially delivering more robust pose estimation models with less labeled data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing the geometric prior of capsule routing with the automated, scalable methodology of network design space exploration, specifically targeting pose estimation through an unsupervised, agreement-based search objective rather than direct supervised training of a fixed capsule model.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Position-Sensitive Pooling for Instance-Level Image Retrieval
proposals.motivation: Large-scale instance-level image retrieval, crucial for applications like visual search and augmented reality, relies on robust and fine-grained feature representations. Current pre-2026 methods, often based on global CNN features or generic local descriptors, struggle with spatial invariance, leading to poor performance on objects with significant viewpoint changes or partial occlusions. A method that explicitly encodes spatial layout without manual annotation is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting the position-sensitive mechanism of R-FCN, originally designed for supervised object detection, within an unsupervised representation learning framework can produce features that are both translation-variant for precise localization and semantically meaningful for robust instance matching.
proposals.method: We will design a self-supervised learning framework where a backbone network (e.g., a RegNet from 'Designing Network Design Spaces') is trained using a contrastive or clustering pretext task on large unlabeled video or image collections, as in 'A Large-Scale Study on Unsupervised Spatiotemporal Representation Learning'. The network's output will feed into a position-sensitive score map layer, inspired by R-FCN, which divides the feature map into spatial grids. For retrieval, we will aggregate these position-aware features using a novel pooling strategy to create a final image descriptor, exploring graph-based matching to refine correspondences.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel unsupervised feature representation explicitly engineered for geometric alignment, advancing the state-of-the-art in instance retrieval benchmarks. It will also provide insights into how object detection architectures can be repurposed for discriminative representation learning without labels, bridging the gap between detection and retrieval communities.
proposals.novelty: While R-FCN is known for detection, its application to unsupervised representation learning for retrieval is unexplored. The core novelty lies in integrating position-sensitive pooling into a label-free neural architecture search or design space exploration, moving beyond simple global feature aggregation.

proposals.title: Unified Position-Aware Representation Learning for Multi-Task Visual Recognition
proposals.motivation: Current multi-task learning frameworks for dense prediction tasks like object detection, keypoint estimation, and attribute prediction often rely on separate, task-specific heads built on a shared backbone. This design can lead to inefficiency and fails to explicitly exploit the fundamental geometric and positional commonalities between these tasks. A unified, position-sensitive representation could enable more parameter-efficient and data-efficient learning, particularly for tasks with limited labeled data.
proposals.hypothesis: A shared, learnable position-sensitive score map, acting as a unified geometric prior, can provide a more effective and efficient foundation for multiple spatially-grounded vision tasks than independent per-task decoders.
proposals.method: We propose to design a novel network architecture where a shared, lightweight sub-network generates a dense 'position map' from a backbone feature pyramid (e.g., ResNet or RegNet). This map encodes generic spatial salience and relative location cues. Task-specific heads for bounding box regression, keypoint heatmap generation, and attribute classification will then use this shared map as a primary input, with minimal additional parameters. We will explore unsupervised or self-supervised pre-training strategies, inspired by spatial pretext tasks, to initialize the position map generator before fine-tuning on multi-task datasets like COCO.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new, parameter-efficient paradigm for multi-task learning based on explicit shared positional representations. It will provide empirical evidence on the benefits of such a design across several standard benchmarks. The resulting architecture and insights will advance the design of simpler, more unified neural networks for complex visual understanding.
proposals.novelty: While positional encodings and task-specific heads are common, the idea of a single, learnable, and shared position map that directly feeds into diverse task decoders is unexplored. This moves beyond simple feature sharing in the backbone to sharing a structured, interpretable intermediate representation explicitly designed for spatial reasoning.

proposals.title: Unified Spatial Pyramid Pooling for Dense Vision Tasks
proposals.motivation: The Spatial Pyramid Pooling (SPP) layer is a cornerstone for handling variable-sized inputs in image classification, enabling fixed-length representations from convolutional features. However, its application beyond classification, particularly in dense prediction tasks like semantic segmentation and human pose estimation, remains underexplored. Extending SPP's multi-scale, resolution-agnostic principle to these tasks could address key challenges in capturing long-range context and multi-scale object information, which are critical for accurate pixel-level and keypoint localization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generalized SPP module, integrated into encoder-decoder and heatmap-based architectures, will significantly improve performance on segmentation and pose estimation by providing robust, multi-scale contextual features without requiring fixed input resolutions. This will lead to more flexible and accurate models.
proposals.method: We will design a generalized SPP module that outputs multi-scale feature maps suitable for dense tasks, not just a pooled vector. For segmentation, we will integrate this module into a U-Net-like decoder, using skip connections to fuse SPP outputs with high-resolution features. For pose estimation, we will incorporate the module before the final heatmap regression head in a SimpleBaseline-like architecture. We will systematically evaluate design choices (pyramid levels, pooling operations, fusion methods) using a neural architecture search-inspired approach on standard benchmarks (COCO, PASCAL VOC).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for applying SPP's strengths to dense vision tasks, demonstrating consistent performance gains. It will yield new, simple architectural blocks for segmentation and pose networks that improve multi-scale reasoning. The resulting design space analysis will offer insights into effective feature pyramid construction beyond classification.
proposals.novelty: While SPP is well-known, its adaptation for generating multi-scale feature *maps* (not just vectors) for dense tasks is a non-trivial extension. The novelty lies in the unified design and systematic NAS-driven exploration of this extended SPP space specifically for segmentation and pose estimation, moving beyond its classic use-case.

proposals.title: Spatial Pyramid Pooling as a Learned, Dynamic Graph for Real-Time Detection
proposals.motivation: Spatial Pyramid Pooling (SPP) modules are crucial for multi-scale feature aggregation in object detection but introduce computational bottlenecks that hinder real-time performance on edge devices. Current SPP implementations use fixed, hand-designed pooling structures, which are inefficient and not optimized for the specific data distribution of a given task. Optimizing this core component is essential for deploying high-accuracy detectors in latency-sensitive applications like autonomous driving and video analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that reformulating the SPP operation as a lightweight, learnable graph structure over feature regions will allow the network to dynamically learn optimal pooling pathways, significantly reducing computational cost while maintaining or improving representation quality for detection.
proposals.method: We will design a novel SPP-Graph module. Instead of fixed pooling kernels and concatenation, we treat feature regions at different grid resolutions as nodes in a graph. A small, learned attention-based network, inspired by graph neural network principles, will predict sparse, adaptive connections between these nodes to perform efficient, context-aware feature aggregation. This module will be integrated and co-optimized within a streamlined detection backbone (like a MobileNet or ShuffleNet variant) using neural architecture search techniques to discover the optimal trade-off between latency and accuracy on COCO and PASCAL VOC datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, efficient SPP variant that reduces latency by over 30% compared to standard SPP in real-time detectors. It will provide a principled framework for viewing feature pooling as a learnable, sparse graph operation, bridging ideas from efficient architecture design and graph representation learning. The resulting models and design space insights will be valuable for the edge AI community.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has optimized backbones and detection heads, the SPP module itself remains a hand-crafted, static component. Our approach is novel in dynamically learning the SPP's internal connectivity as a sparse graph, moving beyond simple kernel size tuning to a fundamentally more adaptive and efficient aggregation scheme.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Pyramid Networks for Unsupervised Video Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Video understanding requires capturing multi-scale features across both space and time. While Spatial Pyramid Pooling (SPP) is a cornerstone for multi-scale spatial feature extraction in images, its direct extension to the temporal dimension for video remains underexplored. This gap limits the ability of modern video architectures to efficiently model actions and events occurring at different temporal granularities, a critical need for robust unsupervised representation learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a principled extension of the spatial pyramid concept into a spatiotemporal pyramid, integrated into a simple 3D CNN or Transformer backbone, will enable more effective and efficient unsupervised learning of multi-scale video representations, outperforming fixed-scale temporal pooling methods.
proposals.method: We will design a Spatiotemporal Pyramid Pooling (STPP) module that performs multi-scale 3D pooling across (height, width, time) dimensions. This module will be integrated into a backbone network like a 3D ResNet or a Video Transformer. We will explore its use within a contrastive or predictive unsupervised learning framework (e.g., extending concepts from SimCLR or BYOL to video). The core design space will systematically vary the number of pyramid levels and the pooling ratios across spatial and temporal axes, guided by principles from 'Designing Network Design Spaces'.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified, flexible module for multi-scale spatiotemporal feature aggregation, directly applicable to unsupervised video learning pipelines. It will establish a new benchmark for efficiency and performance on tasks like video action recognition and temporal localization, demonstrating the value of explicit multi-temporal-scale modeling.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal, systematic adaptation of the spatial pyramid principle to the joint spatiotemporal domain for *unsupervised* learning, moving beyond fixed kernel 3D convolutions or simple temporal pooling. This creates a new, interpretable architectural primitive for video networks.

proposals.title: Deep Networks from Scratch: A Simple, Initialization-Agnostic Training Framework
proposals.motivation: Training very deep neural networks from random initialization remains notoriously unstable, requiring careful, heuristic initialization schemes (e.g., Xavier, Kaiming) and normalization layers (e.g., BatchNorm) to manage gradient flow. This dependency complicates architecture design, limits the exploration of novel, unnormalized structures, and raises questions about the fundamental necessity of these techniques. A method that enables robust training from arbitrary initializations would simplify network design and deepen our understanding of optimization dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically decoupling and re-engineering the components of network forward/backward passes, we can design a simple, architecture-agnostic training framework that achieves stable convergence for very deep networks from any random initialization, without relying on specialized initialization or batch normalization.
proposals.method: We will build upon the principles of 'Designing Network Design Spaces' to construct a new design space focused on training stability rather than final architecture. We will analyze and modify signal propagation using concepts from unsupervised representation learning, treating the forward/backward dynamics as a graph to be regularized. The core method will involve: 1) designing a novel, parameterized activation function that self-normalizes activations and gradients, 2) introducing a simple, per-layer scalar gating mechanism dynamically adjusted during early training to regulate signal magnitude, and 3) validating the approach through large-scale empirical studies on image and video datasets, training ResNet variants and novel, unnormalized architectures from scratch with uniformly random weights.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, simple-to-implement framework for training deep networks that is robust to initialization, reducing the need for heuristic tricks. It will enable more flexible neural architecture search by removing a key constraint (initialization sensitivity) and offer new insights into the fundamental requirements for stable deep network optimization.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on designing good initializations or adding normalization layers, our approach is novel in seeking to make training fundamentally agnostic to initialization through minimal, learnable modifications to the training process itself, treating initialization robustness as an optimizable property.

proposals.title: RegNet-Tiny: Designing and Scaling Efficient Network Design Spaces for Microcontroller-Class Devices
proposals.motivation: The deployment of deep learning on ultra-low-power microcontrollers (MCUs) is critical for pervasive, privacy-preserving edge intelligence, but remains constrained by severe memory and compute limits. Current approaches often rely on ad-hoc scaling of mobile networks (e.g., MobileNetV2) or handcrafted tiny models, lacking a systematic design principle grounded in neural architecture search (NAS) and robust initialization. A principled, automated design space exploration for MCUs is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying the principles of the RegNet design space—characterized by simple, regular networks with quantized linear block and stage configurations—and combining it with parameterized ReLU (PReLU) activations and careful initialization, we can discover a family of models that are both highly accurate and deployable on sub-50KB SRAM MCUs for image recognition.
proposals.method: We will adapt the 'Designing Network Design Spaces' methodology to create a 'RegNet-Tiny' design space with strict FLOPs (≤10M), parameter (≤50K), and activation memory constraints. We will integrate PReLU to improve nonlinearity in very narrow layers. The search will be guided by a hardware-aware cost model for common MCU cores (Cortex-M4/M7). We will employ the unsupervised pre-training strategies from He's spatiotemporal representation learning work to bootstrap models with limited labeled data, followed by supervised fine-tuning on small-scale image datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, Tiny-ImageNet).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available family of Pareto-optimal tiny models for MCUs, alongside a reproducible design space and training recipe. It will provide empirical evidence on the interaction between PReLU, initialization, and extreme model shrinkage, advancing the systematic design of embedded neural networks beyond manual crafting.
proposals.novelty: While NAS for edge devices exists, applying the RegNet design philosophy—which focuses on discovering general network structures—to the extreme resource regime of microcontrollers is novel. The integration of unsupervised pre-training for architecture search in this domain, combined with a focus on activation functions and initialization for stability in tiny models, represents a distinct approach.

proposals.title: RegNAS: Co-Designing Network Capacity and Redundancy Reduction for Negative-Free Self-Supervised Learning
proposals.motivation: Negative-sample-free contrastive learning methods, such as those based on redundancy reduction (e.g., Barlow Twins) or uniformity (e.g., SimSiam), offer simpler and more scalable self-supervised learning (SSL) by avoiding costly negative mining. However, they are prone to representational collapse or suboptimal performance if the model's architectural capacity is not carefully matched to the task and loss dynamics. Current SSL research largely treats architecture design as a fixed, pre-defined choice, separate from the optimization of the learning objective.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamically co-optimizing the architectural capacity (cardinality) of a network's embedding spaces with a negative-free contrastive loss will yield more stable, efficient, and performant representations by preventing collapse and automatically adapting model complexity to data.
proposals.method: We will develop a unified framework where the embedding layers are parameterized by a structured design space, inspired by RegNet principles, allowing for adaptive control over width, depth, and bottleneck ratios. This architectural subspace will be optimized jointly with a negative-free loss (e.g., Barlow Twins or VICReg) using a gradient-based Neural Architecture Search (NAS) strategy. The loss signal will guide the search to expand capacity where redundancy is high and contract it where representations are stable, creating a feedback loop between architecture and learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a principled method for architecture-aware negative-free SSL, demonstrating improved stability and sample efficiency over fixed-architecture baselines. It will also contribute a new perspective on NAS for unsupervised learning, showing that optimal architectures are loss-dependent and can be discovered without labels.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored NAS for supervised tasks and fixed architectures for SSL, the co-optimization of network cardinality specifically for collapse-prone, negative-free objectives is a novel and non-obvious direction. It fuses the design space philosophy of RegNet with the algorithmic simplicity of modern redundancy-reduction losses.

proposals.title: ResNeXt as a Dense Prediction Scaffold: A Modular Optimization Framework for Segmentation
proposals.motivation: ResNeXt's grouped convolution design offers a powerful, parameter-efficient backbone for image classification, but its systematic evaluation and optimization for dense prediction tasks like semantic and instance segmentation remain underexplored. The field often treats classification backbones as generic feature extractors without task-specific architectural refinement, potentially leaving significant performance gains untapped. This work addresses this gap by formalizing a principled, scaffold-based adaptation methodology inspired by modular optimization pipelines in other computational domains.
proposals.hypothesis: The ResNeXt architecture, treated as a validated scaffold, can be systematically optimized for segmentation by tuning its core modular hyperparameters—cardinality and width—in a task-aware manner, leading to more efficient and performant dense prediction networks than using off-the-shelf classification-optimized versions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-phase study. First, we establish baselines by integrating standard ResNeXt variants (e.g., 32x4d, 64x4d) into established segmentation heads (e.g., DeepLabV3+, Mask R-CNN) on datasets like COCO and Cityscapes. Second, we perform a targeted ablation study, treating cardinality and width as tunable modules. Using a neural architecture search (NAS) framework guided by segmentation-specific metrics (e.g., mIoU, boundary F1), we will search for optimal configurations of these parameters under a fixed computational budget, explicitly testing the trade-offs unique to dense pixel-wise prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a systematic benchmark of ResNeXt for modern segmentation tasks, 2) a set of segmentation-optimized ResNeXt configurations, and 3) a generalizable, scaffold-based methodology for adapting proven architectural patterns to new problem domains, moving beyond simple transfer learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in framing architecture adaptation through the explicit analogy of a modular drug discovery pipeline, which structures the optimization process. Instead of a black-box NAS, we perform a constrained, interpretable search over the core, pre-defined modular dimensions of a successful scaffold, a focused approach not yet applied to ResNeXt for segmentation.

proposals.title: ResNeXt for Dense Prediction: A Systematic Evaluation on Semantic and Instance Segmentation
proposals.motivation: While ResNeXt has demonstrated superior performance over ResNet on image classification, its efficacy on more complex, dense prediction tasks like semantic and instance segmentation remains underexplored. A systematic evaluation is crucial, as these tasks are fundamental to advanced vision systems (e.g., autonomous driving, medical imaging) and may benefit differently from the cardinality-based grouping in ResNeXt compared to deeper or wider networks. Establishing best practices for adapting ResNeXt backbones for segmentation would provide valuable guidance to the community.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the multi-branch, grouped convolution design of ResNeXt provides a more effective feature representation for dense prediction tasks than standard ResNet, leading to higher accuracy and better efficiency when integrated into modern segmentation frameworks like Mask R-CNN and DeepLabv3+.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled empirical study, using ResNet-50/101 and their ResNeXt-50/101 (32x4d) counterparts as backbones within established segmentation frameworks. For instance segmentation, we will integrate them into Mask R-CNN. For semantic segmentation, we will use DeepLabv3+ with an ASPP module. All models will be pre-trained on ImageNet and fine-tuned on standard benchmarks (COCO for instance segmentation, Cityscapes and PASCAL VOC for semantic segmentation). We will rigorously compare performance (mAP, mIoU), computational cost (FLOPs), and parameter efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive benchmark of ResNeXt for major segmentation tasks, establishing clear performance baselines and adaptation guidelines. It will yield insights into how architectural properties like cardinality interact with segmentation head designs, informing future backbone architecture selection and design for dense prediction.
proposals.novelty: Despite ResNeXt's prominence, prior work has not systematically isolated and evaluated its impact beyond classification; this study directly addresses that gap. The analysis will specifically probe whether the gains from grouped convolutions translate effectively to pixel-level localization and mask prediction, which is non-obvious given the different feature requirements.

proposals.title: Cardinality-Aware Contrastive Learning: A Unified Framework for Adaptive and Efficient Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: In self-supervised contrastive learning (e.g., MoCo, SimCLR), the cardinality of negative samples is crucial for learning quality representations, but scaling it naively in end-to-end training is computationally prohibitive. While methods like MoCo's memory queue address this, they use a fixed, heuristically chosen size, lacking a principled link between the theoretical benefit of cardinality and practical training efficiency. This gap prevents optimal resource allocation and limits the accessibility of high-performance self-supervised learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formalizing the diminishing returns of cardinality on representation quality will allow us to design adaptive training algorithms that dynamically adjust the effective cardinality, thereby achieving superior performance-efficiency trade-offs compared to fixed-cardinality baselines.
proposals.method: We will first formalize the relationship between cardinality, model capacity, and downstream task performance through a controlled empirical study, establishing a principle of diminishing returns. Guided by this, we will design novel algorithms for end-to-end frameworks like MoCo v3. These include gradient-aware negative mining, which prioritizes hard negatives based on gradient norms, and a progressive queue scheduler that increases queue size as a function of training loss plateauing, rather than a fixed schedule. We will validate these methods within a structured network design space to isolate architectural interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a theoretically-grounded, practical framework for efficient contrastive learning, moving beyond heuristic queue sizes. It will deliver new adaptive algorithms that set a state-of-the-art performance-efficiency Pareto frontier and offer clear design principles for scaling self-supervised learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct fusion of empirical scaling laws with dynamic training algorithm design for contrastive learning, creating a closed-loop system where theory directly guides efficient resource allocation, an approach not explored in prior work on memory banks or static architectures.

proposals.title: ResNeXt3D-FAA: A Factorized and Axial-Attentional Architecture for Efficient Spatiotemporal Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Processing 3D data (e.g., video, volumetric medical images) with deep networks is computationally prohibitive, limiting model depth and applicability. Pure 3D convolutional networks like 3D ResNet/ResNeXt suffer from parameter explosion and struggle with long-range dependencies. An efficient, scalable architecture that balances local pattern extraction with global context modeling is critically needed for large-scale 3D understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a ResNeXt-inspired 3D block, combining highly factorized grouped convolutions for local efficiency with a lightweight axial attention mechanism for global context, will yield a more parameter- and compute-efficient architecture for unsupervised spatiotemporal representation learning than standard 3D CNNs or pure attention models.
proposals.method: We design a novel block replacing the standard 3D convolution in a ResNeXt-style network. First, a factorized 3D grouped convolution decomposes the operation into spatial and temporal grouped convolutions to drastically reduce parameters. Second, a streamlined axial attention module is applied selectively across the grouped feature channels, processing spatial and temporal axes sequentially to capture long-range dependencies with linear complexity. The architecture will be evaluated within a design space framework and trained using contrastive or pretext-task-based unsupervised learning on large-scale video datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, scalable backbone for 3D computer vision that directly addresses the efficiency bottlenecks of prior work. It will provide empirical evidence on the synergistic combination of factorized convolutions and axial attention within a multi-path grouped design. The resulting models will enable more practical application of deep learning to high-resolution 3D data where computational cost is a primary constraint.
proposals.novelty: The integration of axial attention as a complementary pathway within a factorized ResNeXt3D block is novel, creating a hybrid local-global block specifically optimized for 3D efficiency. This moves beyond simply applying attention after CNNs or using pure transformer architectures for video.

proposals.title: ResNeXt3D-Vol: Fractal-Inspired Multi-Scale Pathways for Volumetric Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Processing 3D volumetric data (e.g., video, medical scans) is computationally demanding, and standard 3D CNNs often struggle to efficiently capture multi-scale spatial-temporal structures. Drawing inspiration from geophysical seismic inversion, which fuses multi-scale interpretations to build robust 3D models, suggests a powerful architectural principle for neural networks. Adapting this principle could lead to more parameter-efficient and performant models for critical applications like video understanding and medical image analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: A 3D convolutional network block that explicitly aggregates features from parallel pathways, each operating at a distinct spatial scale via controlled dilation or striding, will enable more effective and efficient hierarchical representation learning from volumetric data than standard monolithic 3D convolutions.
proposals.method: We will design a new 'ResNeXt3D-Vol' block, extending the grouped convolution (cardinality) concept of ResNeXt into the volumetric domain. Within each block, multiple parallel pathways will apply 3D convolutions with pathway-specific dilation rates or stride-1 convolutions on differently pre-subsampled inputs to achieve intrinsic multi-scale processing. The block output will be formed by aggregating pathway outputs via summation or channel-wise concatenation. These blocks will be integrated into a 3D ResNet-style design space, which we will then analyze and optimize using automated network design or simple, label-efficient Neural Architecture Search principles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, principled 3D convolutional building block that embeds multi-scale feature extraction, improving model capacity without a quadratic parameter increase. It will deliver strong empirical results on benchmarks for video action recognition (e.g., Kinetics) and 3D medical image segmentation (e.g., BraTS). The work will also demonstrate the value of cross-domain analogies, from computational geophysics to neural architecture design.
proposals.novelty: While multi-scale networks and 3D ResNeXt exist, the specific design of a *single block* with parallel, fixed, and distinct spatial-scale pathways, directly inspired by seismic processing workflows, is novel. This creates a fractal-like, in-stage multi-resolution analysis distinct from sequential or pyramidal multi-scale approaches.

proposals.title: Task-Adaptive Cardinality Search for Efficient 3D ResNeXt Architectures
proposals.motivation: 3D convolutional networks like 3D ResNeXt are powerful for volumetric data analysis in video understanding and medical imaging, but their high computational cost, partly from fixed, high cardinality, limits deployment in resource-constrained settings like clinics. Current NAS methods often optimize entire architectures from scratch, neglecting efficient adaptation of proven, powerful base topologies. A method to tailor the cardinality dimension of established 3D networks to specific task demands is needed for practical efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: The optimal cardinality (number of parallel paths) in a 3D ResNeXt block is task-dependent and layer-specific, and a systematic search can identify a sparser, more efficient configuration with minimal loss in representational power for a given volumetric task.
proposals.method: We will develop a differentiable neural architecture search (NAS) strategy, inspired by DARTS, that treats the cardinality of each 3D ResNeXt block as a continuous, optimizable variable. A supernet encompassing a range of cardinalities per block will be constructed. Using a task-specific loss (e.g., for action recognition on Kinetics), we will learn a set of architectural weights that soft-select among parallel paths, followed by discretization to derive a final, task-adapted network with heterogeneous, reduced cardinality. This will be complemented by a sensitivity analysis to validate the necessity of retained paths.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a principled method for generating task-adapted, efficient 3D ConvNets from a powerful base design, significantly reducing FLOPs and parameters. It will provide empirical insights into the relationship between task complexity, spatial-temporal features, and architectural cardinality. The resulting models will democratize high-quality 3D analysis for edge and clinical applications.
proposals.novelty: While NAS for 3D networks exists, this work uniquely focuses on optimizing only the cardinality dimension of the established and effective 3D ResNeXt template, creating a direct path from a heavy model to a lean, task-specialized variant, which is more data- and compute-efficient than full architecture search.

ideas.title: Efficient ResNeXt3D with Factorized Convolutions and Axial Attention
ideas.description: This adversarially redesigned architecture adapts the ResNeXt philosophy for 3D data while directly combating its computational and representational flaws. It employs a two-component block: first, a factorized 3D grouped convolution drastically reduces parameters while capturing local spatial and temporal patterns. Second, a streamlined axial attention module, applied selectively across the grouped channels, captures essential long-range dependencies within the volume or video sequence. This hybrid design maintains the multi-path, grouped structure of ResNeXt but ensures scalability and effectiveness for large 3D datasets like high-resolution medical scans or long video clips, where pure 3D convolution fails.

ideas.title: Cardinality-Aware 3D ResNeXt: Task-Adaptive and Efficient Volumetric Networks
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the architectural power of 3D ResNeXt with a systematic search for minimal necessary complexity. It proposes a method to determine the optimal cardinality for each layer or block within a 3D convolutional network based on the specific demands of a given volumetric task (e.g., video action recognition, MRI tumor segmentation). By developing a sensitivity analysis or a differentiable search strategy, the work aims to prune or consolidate parallel paths in the 3D ResNeXt architecture that contribute marginally to performance for that task. The outcome is a family of task-adapted 3D models that are significantly more parameter- and compute-efficient than their densely cardinal counterparts, making high-quality 3D analysis more feasible in resource-constrained environments like clinical settings or embedded systems.

ideas.title: Adapting Modular Drug Discovery Pipelines to Extend ResNeXt for Segmentation Tasks
ideas.description: This idea proposes extending ResNeXt evaluation to tasks like semantic and instance segmentation by drawing an analogy from computational drug discovery. In that field, a validated molecular scaffold is adapted for new diseases via modular testing and side-chain optimization. Adapted to computer vision, we treat ResNeXt as a proven architectural scaffold. The research would involve a two-phase process: first, a systematic evaluation by plugging ResNeXt into standard segmentation network heads to establish baseline performance; second, a targeted ablation study to fine-tune its core modular parameters (cardinality and width) specifically for the demands of dense prediction tasks, thereby optimizing the scaffold for the new 'therapeutic area' of segmentation without discarding its fundamental, powerful structure.

ideas.title: ResNeXt3D-Vol: Multi-Scale Feature Aggregation for Volumetric Data via Fractal-Inspired Pathways
ideas.description: This research adapts the ResNeXt architecture for 3D volumetric data (video, medical CT/MRI) by drawing an analogy from geophysical seismic processing. In seismology, robust 3D subsurface models are built using multi-scale, multi-path computational inversions that fuse coarse and fine interpretations. We translate this solution by designing a new 3D convolutional block, 'ResNeXt3D-Vol'. This block contains several parallel pathways, each applying 3D convolutions to the input volume at a distinct spatial scale—achieved through pathway-specific dilation rates or striding. The outputs of these parallel multi-scale transformations are aggregated via summation or concatenation. This creates an intrinsic, fractal-inspired multi-resolution feature extraction within each network stage, significantly improving the model's capacity to hierarchically learn from complex 3D structures without a proportional increase in parameters, making it highly suitable for tasks like video action recognition or tumor segmentation in medical volumes.

ideas.title: Performance-Guaranteed Efficient End-to-End Contrastive Learning
ideas.description: This research develops a resource-adaptive framework for end-to-end MoCo that maintains a strict performance floor while pursuing efficiency. The system integrates a small, fixed-capacity auxiliary network performing a self-supervised proxy task to continuously monitor representation quality during training. It employs a controller that dynamically adjusts computational costs—such as the number of negative samples, gradient flow paths, or queue update frequency—based on real-time feedback from this auxiliary task, preventing harmful degradation. The outcome is a training procedure that is provably more efficient than standard end-to-end MoCo while matching or exceeding its downstream task accuracy.

ideas.title: Adaptive Cardinality for Negative-Free Contrastive Learning
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework that co-optimizes the architectural capacity of a model's embedding layers with a negative-sample-free contrastive loss. The core idea is to dynamically adjust the cardinality (dimensionality and complexity) of different embedding spaces based on the learning signal from a loss that does not rely on hard negative mining, such as a redundancy reduction or uniformity-based objective. This adaptive capacity prevents the representational collapse that typically plagues negative-free methods, as the model can expand or contract its representational power in response to the data's inherent complexity and the loss's requirements. The result is a more efficient and stable self-supervised system that automatically tailors its architecture to the task's difficulty without the computational and algorithmic overhead of curating negative samples.

ideas.title: Theoretically-Guided, Efficient Cardinality Scaling for End-to-End Contrastive Learning
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework that marries the theoretical understanding of cardinality's benefits with the practical constraints of end-to-end training efficiency. We first formalize the relationship between cardinality, model capacity, and downstream task performance, establishing theoretical principles for diminishing returns. Using these principles, we then design novel, adaptive training algorithms for frameworks like end-to-end MoCo that dynamically adjust the effective cardinality during training—for example, through intelligent negative mining, progressive queue sizing, or gradient-aware sampling—rather than using a fixed, large queue. This ensures computational and memory resources are allocated optimally, maximizing the performance gain per unit of compute, which neither pure theory nor isolated efficiency techniques could achieve.

ideas.title: ResNeXt-Micro: Ultra-Low Cardinality Networks via Chemical Bonding Analogy
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from molecular chemistry, where minimal atoms form robust structures through strategic bonding, this research adapts the concept to neural architecture design. The analogous solution from chemistry is using strong, specific covalent bonds (single, double, triple) between a minimal set of atoms to create stable, functional molecules, where properties emerge from bond type and geometry rather than sheer atomic quantity. The adapted idea, 'ResNeXt-Micro,' proposes designing extremely low-cardinality ResNeXt variants (e.g., cardinality of 2-4) where the grouped convolutions are treated as 'atoms.' Instead of uniform connections, we introduce 'convolutional bonds'—hybrid connection modules that combine these ultra-narrow groups with varying degrees of feature sharing, gating, and non-linear activation, mimicking different bond strengths and types. This approach aims to generate a highly expressive and efficient feature transformation network from minimal components, specifically optimized for the computational and memory constraints of mobile and embedded vision systems, achieving performance through intelligent architectural bonding rather than increased width or cardinality.

ideas.title: Non-Collapsing Asymmetric Contrastive Learning with Uniformity Regularization
ideas.description: This system learns effective representations without explicit negative sample mining by combining an asymmetric online-target network architecture with a predictor and stop-gradient to prevent representational collapse. It adds a uniformity loss term that penalizes the concentration of embeddings on the unit hypersphere, ensuring they are well-separated and cover the space effectively. This dual approach provides the necessary repulsive force implicitly, maintaining training stability and high performance. The method is validated on image and graph benchmarks, showing it matches or exceeds the performance of negative-dependent contrastive methods.

ideas.title: Differentiable Search for Task-Optimal Heterogeneous Cardinality Networks
ideas.description: This research develops a framework that automates the design of heterogeneous cardinality architectures by learning the cardinality schedule directly from data. A small, differentiable policy network analyzes the evolving feature maps and predicts a soft cardinality value for each block, which is used to weight the outputs of parallel transformations with different group sizes. The policy network and the main network are trained jointly via gradient descent, enabling the discovery of complex, non-monotonic cardinality patterns tailored to specific datasets and computational budgets. This approach transforms cardinality from a static, hand-designed hyperparameter into a dynamically optimized architectural dimension.

ideas.title: Topologically Heterogeneous Dynamic Cardinality Networks
ideas.description: This research direction proposes neural architectures where each layer's set of transformations (e.g., convolutional filters, attention heads) is explicitly composed of subgroups with distinct topological properties—such as spherical, toroidal, or hyperbolic geometries—governing their parameter interactions. Crucially, the cardinality (size) of each of these topologically distinct subgroups is not uniform but is strategically and dynamically varied across layers, creating a heterogeneous cardinality profile that is aligned with the topological complexity required at different depths of the network. This allows the model to allocate more parameters to transformation topologies suited for capturing fine-grained, hierarchical, or long-range dependencies where needed, while economizing on simpler topologies elsewhere. The result is a network whose fundamental building blocks and their population are co-designed, enabling more efficient and expressive learning of complex, multi-scale data manifolds.

ideas.title: Diversity-Constrained Attention for Robust Path Combination
ideas.description: This system employs a multi-head attention module to combine features from multiple transformation paths, but it is explicitly designed to prevent collapse. Each attention head is regularized via an auxiliary loss to maintain high mutual information with a specific transformation type. Furthermore, a batch-wise diversity loss minimizes the similarity between the attention weight matrices across heads, ensuring different paths are utilized. This results in a robust, learned combination of transformations that demonstrably leverages multiple input pathways, improving generalization and representation richness.

ideas.title: Adaptive Group Convolution via Neural Architecture Search Primitives
ideas.description: This idea challenges the fixed group size assumption in grouped convolutions by drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology, where the effective population size is a dynamic outcome of complex interactions, not a fixed input. The analogous solution involves estimating this emergent property from observable genetic data. Adapted to deep learning, we propose making group size learnable per layer by implementing a differentiable neural architecture search primitive. A controller network or a set of gating parameters would operate on a densely connected initial state, applying a sparsity constraint to the adjacency between filter groups. This allows the network to dynamically discover and adapt the effective group convolution size—both the cardinality (how many groups) and the membership (which filters belong together)—as a function of the layer's depth, the task, and the input data, moving beyond a rigid, hand-designed hyperparameter.

ideas.title: Momentum-Guided Automated Cardinality Selection
ideas.description: This research proposes to develop a unified framework where the momentum term in an optimizer is not just a fixed schedule for accelerating gradients, but a learned, adaptive signal used to automatically determine the optimal cardinality (e.g., width, number of filters, experts) for different network components. The core synergy is that the momentum's behavior—capturing the inertia of the optimization path—serves as a real-time, task-specific heuristic for whether a layer needs more or less capacity. This allows the model's architecture and its training dynamics to co-evolve, leading to more efficient neural networks where resource allocation is dynamically tuned based on the inherent difficulty and stability of learning different representations.

ideas.title: Adaptive Momentum Buffer via Reservoir Sampling for Efficient End-to-End MoCo
ideas.description: To improve the memory and computational efficiency of end-to-end MoCo training, we draw an analogy from streaming data algorithms, specifically reservoir sampling used in network monitoring. The analogous solution involves maintaining a fixed-size, statistically representative sample from an unbounded data stream without storing it all. We adapt this by replacing MoCo's large, shifting momentum queue with an adaptive momentum buffer of fixed capacity. New embeddings from the momentum encoder are fed into this buffer using a reservoir sampling protocol, where each new candidate replaces a random existing buffer element with a probability proportional to the buffer's capacity and the number of samples processed. This ensures the buffer contains a uniformly sampled set of past embeddings, preserving negative sample diversity for effective contrastive loss while imposing a strict, constant memory footprint. This eliminates the computational cost of queue manipulation and reduces GPU memory pressure, making end-to-end training more scalable.

ideas.title: Dynamic Routing with Inverted Aggregation for Hierarchical Feature Disentanglement
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel neural architecture module that integrates capsule network routing with a learnable, inverted aggregation step. The dynamic routing algorithm assigns input features to specialized transformation paths, each designed to capture a specific entity or property. Crucially, the outputs of these paths are not simply summed; instead, a secondary routing mechanism or gating network selects from a set of candidate aggregation operations (e.g., product, weighted mean, attention-based fusion) to combine them. This allows the model to explicitly learn the compositional relationships between discovered entities, moving beyond additive feature combination to model more complex interactions like occlusion, multiplicative modulation, or hierarchical part-whole binding.

ideas.title: Adaptively Gated Input Paths with Divergence Regularization
ideas.description: This redesigned system allows each transformation path to receive a gated, specialized version of the input through a learnable attention or masking mechanism. Crucially, to prevent catastrophic divergence, it introduces a consistency regularization loss that penalizes excessive dissimilarity between the intermediate feature maps produced by each path after a few layers. This enables paths to develop complementary processing strategies—such as focusing on different spatial frequencies or feature channels—while maintaining alignment towards a common representational goal. The gating parameters and the strength of the regularization are jointly optimized, allowing the model to discover the optimal trade-off between specialization and coherence.

ideas.title: Hybrid Self-Supervised Learning via a Multi-Task Cognitive Architecture
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from cognitive neuroscience, where robust skill acquisition combines declarative and procedural learning pathways, this idea proposes a hybrid self-supervised learning architecture. The analogous solution involves the brain's multi-system engagement for flexible expertise. Adapted to MoCo, this means integrating the contrastive learning objective (procedural) with a generative pretext task like masked autoencoding (declarative) into a single multi-task framework. The model uses a shared encoder trained with a combined, potentially adaptive, loss function. This forces the learned representations to simultaneously excel at discriminating between instances and reconstructing corrupted inputs, leading to more powerful, transferable, and semantically rich unsupervised features by satisfying a broader set of constraints.

ideas.title: Asymmetric Contrastive Learning via Sensory Pathway Analogy
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience and sensory processing, this idea challenges the symmetry assumption in contrastive learning encoders. In biological systems, like the visual pathway, information flow is inherently asymmetric: retinal cells perform initial, specialized compression (e.g., edge detection), while cortical areas build complex, integrative representations. The analogous solution is to embrace this functional asymmetry rather than enforce symmetry. Adapted to machine learning, this proposes an asymmetric contrastive framework where the key encoder is a shallow, fixed (or slowly updated) network acting as a specialized 'feature receptor,' producing stable, local anchors. The query encoder is a deeper, trainable network acting as a 'context integrator,' learning to map these anchors into a rich, contextualized embedding space. The training objective is modified to align these asymmetric representations, potentially improving efficiency and the quality of learned hierarchies by mirroring successful biological design principles.

ideas.title: Dynamic Architecture Scaling with Learned Cardinality via Attention
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel neural architecture where a large set of parallel transformation paths (high cardinality) of varying depths and widths are defined as a superset of components. A learned attention mechanism, conditioned on the input or intermediate representations, is used to softly select and combine outputs from this superset. This approach systematically studies how the model learns to allocate computational resources (activating deeper, wider, or more numerous paths) based on data complexity, creating an empirical, dynamic scaling law. The core synergy is that attention provides the mechanism to make cardinality, depth, and width active, learnable dimensions of the model rather than static design choices.
